articles.title: Trump Wants to Revive Shipping. Investors Are Slow to Back Him.
articles.abstract: A French logistics behemoth promised $20 billion for the United States, but a year into President Trump’s second term, only a fraction of the money has arrived.

multimedia.caption: “He’s going to be investing $20 billion into the United States because of the election,” President Trump said last year when Rodolphe Saadé, the chief executive of the shipping company CMA CGM, visited the White House.
multimedia.copyright: Maansi Srivastava for The New York Times

articles.title: Ford Says Electric Vehicle Losses Will Continue for 3 More Years
articles.abstract: Ford Motor reported a big loss for 2025 because of its troubled electric vehicle division, which it has significantly scaled back.

multimedia.caption: An F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck, which the company stopped producing last year, after federal tax credits for electric vehicles ended.

articles.title: Epstein Files Reveal Efforts to Build Ties With Officials in Russia
articles.abstract: New documents detail Jeffrey Epstein’s efforts to foster strategic, sometimes reciprocal relationships with Russian officials.
articles.byline: By Steven Lee Myers and Nataliya Vasilyeva

multimedia.caption: The latest released files show that Jeffrey Epstein had hoped to facilitate a meeting with the Russian president, Vladimir V. Putin.
multimedia.copyright: Doug Mills/The New York Times

articles.title: Top Labor Regulator Dismisses Complaint Against SpaceX
articles.abstract: The National Labor Relations Board, having accused the company of unfair retaliation in 2024, now says it has no jurisdiction over Elon Musk’s space company.
articles.byline: By Noam Scheiber and Ryan Mac

multimedia.caption: Eight SpaceX employees were fired soon after asking the company to distance itself from comments made by Elon Musk, including one in which he made light of accusations of harassment directed at him.
multimedia.copyright: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times

articles.title: New Epstein Details Rattle Washington, Hollywood and Beyond
articles.abstract: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick and Casey Wasserman, the entertainment mogul, are among those facing blowback amid the release of files related to Jeffrey Epstein.
articles.byline: By Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe

multimedia.caption: Representatives Ro Khanna, Democrat of California, and Thomas Massie, Republican of Kentucky, are part of a bipartisan political push to shed more light on the Jeffrey Epstein ordeal.

articles.title: BP Suspends Share Buybacks as Profit Slumps
articles.abstract: The British energy giant said it would put money toward new oil and gas opportunities ahead of the arrival of its new chief executive, Meg O’Neill.

multimedia.caption: BP is turning back to its traditional oil and gas business in an effort to revive its fortunes.

articles.title: Operator of Eddie Bauer Stores Files for Bankruptcy
articles.abstract: The company that operates the brand’s retail stores cited a shift in consumer preferences, rising inflation and elevated tariffs.

multimedia.caption: Eddie Bauer’s retail operator has said it would seek to sell 180 stores across the United States and Canada.
multimedia.copyright: Andrew Spear for The New York Times

articles.title: Watch: A Conversation With Anna Wintour and Her U.S. Vogue Successor, Chloe Malle
articles.abstract: In their first joint interview, Ms. Malle spoke about filling the shoes of Ms. Wintour, who urged people to “get over comparisons.”
articles.byline: By Jessica Testa, Chevaz Clarke, Daniel Fetherston and Lauren Pruitt

articles.title: Anna Wintour and Chloe Malle on the Future of Vogue
articles.abstract: In their first joint interview, Anna Wintour and Chloe Malle discuss the future of American Vogue. Ms. Malle, who succeeded Ms. Wintour as editor in September, shared how she plans to put her own stamp on the magazine.
articles.byline: By Jessica Testa, Chevaz Clarke and Daniel Fetherston

articles.title: Pilots and Flight Attendants Criticize American Airlines’ Performance
articles.abstract: The unions representing pilots and flight attendants have in recent days publicly criticized American for falling behind Delta Air Lines and United Airlines.

articles.title: Court TV, Known for Live Trial Coverage, Is Getting a New Owner
articles.abstract: E.W. Scripps is selling the channel to the parent company of Law&Crime, which wants Court TV to have a bigger streaming presence.

articles.title: A.I. Blitzes the Big Game
articles.abstract: Ads pitching artificial intelligence companies dominated the Super Bowl broadcast. Their huge cost probably didn’t ease investor worries about spending.
articles.byline: By Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount, Grady McGregor and Lauren Hirsch

multimedia.caption: The large number of A.I. ads featured during the Super Bowl LX broadcast may go down as more memorable than the game itself.

articles.title: From Fishing Nets to Furniture: Turning Ocean Plastic Into Usable Products
articles.abstract: A brother-and-sister team in Spain started a sustainable business model recycling old and damaged nets into stadium seating and decorations.
articles.byline: By Lena Mucha For The New York Times

articles.title: Investors Give an Exuberant Welcome to Takaichi’s Big Win in Japan
articles.abstract: Stocks climbed on Monday as investors cheered a result seen as a mandate for the prime minister’s high-spending economic agenda.

multimedia.caption: Sanae Takaichi is broadly viewed as a boon for corporate Japan because of her support for low interest rates and robust fiscal spending to stimulate growth.

articles.title: Why the N.F.L. Booked Bad Bunny for the Super Bowl
articles.abstract: League executives may be nervous about the Latin superstar’s outspoken stance on immigration, but their priority is attracting popular halftime performers.

articles.title: MAGA’s Split Over Israel Extends to a Ship Attacked 58 Years Ago
articles.abstract: The Israeli military killed 34 people on the U.S.S. Liberty in 1967. Whether it was an accident, as many historians believe, has become a litmus test within President Trump’s movement.
des_facet: United States Defense and Military Forces

articles.title: Feeling ‘Amateur’ at Retirement Planning, They Asked A.I. for Help
articles.abstract: More people are turning to chatbots for advice, inquiring about high-stakes decisions such as saving for retirement — even if they eventually turn to a human.

multimedia.caption: Caitlyn Yingling used ChatGPT to help guide significant changes to her and her husband’s retirement accounts.
